title: Check if path sum exists
description: Determine if there exists a root-to-leaf path such that the sum of node values equals a given target

examples.explanation:
1. Start at root 5 with sum=0 → sum=5
2. Go to left child 4 → sum=5+4=9
3. Go to left child 11 → sum=9+11=20
4. Go to left child 7 → sum=20+7=27 (leaf, 27≠22, return false)
5. Backtrack to 11, go to right child 2 → sum=20+2=22 (leaf, 22=22, return true)
6. Since true found in right subtree, propagate true up → final result true

1. Start at root 1 with sum=0 → sum=1
2. Go to left child 2 → sum=1+2=3 (leaf, 3≠5, return false)
3. Backtrack to 1, go to right child 3 → sum=1+3=4 (leaf, 4≠5, return false)
4. Both subtrees return false → final result false

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively traverse all root-to-leaf paths, checking if any path's sum equals the target. This is straightforward and explores every possible path.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (worst-case O(n) for skewed trees)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Path Sum Exists Using Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if any root-to-leaf path sum equals target using recursive traversal.

explanation.prose: This is the complete implementation of the path sum problem using brute force recursion. The intuition is to systematically explore every possible root-to-leaf path in the binary tree, accumulating node values along each path, and checking if the accumulated sum equals the target when reaching a leaf node. The algorithm works by performing a depth-first traversal: starting from the root with an initial sum of 0, we recursively add each node's value to the running sum as we traverse downward. When we reach a leaf node (both children are null), we check if the accumulated sum equals the target. If yes, we return true; otherwise, we continue exploring other paths. The key insight is that we need to explore ALL possible paths from root to leaves, which this brute force approach guarantees by recursively checking both left and right subtrees. The .

timeComplexity.note: where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node exactly once

spaceComplexity.note: where h is the tree height, due to the recursion stack (worst-case O(n) for skewed trees)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Path Sum Checker Function
ladders.desc: The recursive helper function that checks if a path from current node to leaf equals target.

explanation.prose: This is the core recursive function that implements the path sum checking logic. The intuition is that at each node, we want to check if there exists a path from this node to any leaf that sums to the remaining target. We do this by subtracting the current node's value from the target and recursively checking the left and right children. The algorithm: 1. Base case: if node is null, return false (no path exists). 2. Base case: if node is a leaf (both children null), check if node.value equals remaining target. 3. Recursive case: compute new target = target - node.value, then recursively check left and right subtrees with this new target. The function returns true if either subtree returns true. It connects to L2 as it implements the core recursive logic.

connectsTo.reason: by being the main recursive function called from the wrapper

timeComplexity.note: as we visit each node once

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Tree Traversal with Accumulated Sum
ladders.desc: The recursive pattern of traversing tree while maintaining running sum.

explanation.prose: This level focuses on the core recursive technique of traversing a binary tree while accumulating a sum. The intuition is that we need to explore all paths from root to leaves, which naturally suggests a depth-first traversal. At each recursive call, we maintain a 'current sum' that represents the sum of values from root to the current node. When we move to a child node, we add that child's value to the current sum. The key insight is that this accumulated sum gets passed down the recursion, and when we reach a leaf, we have the complete path sum to check against target. The algorithm: 1. Start with current sum = 0 at root. 2. At each node: add node.value to current sum. 3. If node is leaf: check if current sum equals target. 4. If not leaf: recursively traverse left and right children with updated current sum. It connects to L3 as it builds on basic tree traversal.

connectsTo.reason: as it's the traversal pattern used in the helper function

commonMistakes: forgetting to pass updated sum to recursive calls
not resetting sum when backtracking (not needed here as sum is passed by value)
or incorrect leaf detection. The real pattern is traverse(node
current_sum) with recursive calls to children

2. Go left to 2: current_sum=1+2=3 (leaf, 3≠4 → false)
3. Backtrack, go right to 3: current_sum=1+3=4 (leaf, 4=4 → true)
4. Return true

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Tree Traversal (DFS)
ladders.desc: Depth-first traversal of binary tree to visit all nodes.

explanation.prose: This level covers the fundamental skill of traversing a binary tree using depth-first search (DFS). The intuition is that to check all root-to-leaf paths, we need to systematically visit every node in the tree, which DFS accomplishes by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. For a binary tree, DFS typically means recursively visiting the current node, then its left subtree, then its right subtree (pre-order traversal). The algorithm: 1. If node is null, return (base case). 2. Process current node (e.g., print value or check condition). 3. Recursively traverse left subtree. 4. Recursively traverse right subtree. It connects to L4 as it uses recursive function calls.

connectsTo.reason: as traversal is needed to implement the path sum checking

commonMistakes: infinite recursion by missing base case
or not handling null children. The real pattern is dfs(node) with recursive calls to dfs(node.left)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Calls with Parameters
ladders.desc: Creating recursive functions that pass and update parameters.

explanation.prose: This level covers the programming construct of recursive function calls with parameters, which is essential for the path sum algorithm. The intuition is that recursion allows a function to call itself with modified parameters, enabling us to break down a complex problem (checking all paths) into smaller subproblems (checking subtrees). In the path sum context, we need to pass both the current node and the remaining target sum to each recursive call. The algorithm pattern: 1. Define a function f(node, target). 2. Base case(s) handle simplest scenarios (null node or leaf). 3. Recursive case: modify parameters (e.g., target - node.value) and call f on child nodes. 4. Combine results from recursive calls (e.g., using OR for path existence). Time/space depends on implementation but generally O(n) time It connects to L5 as it builds on basic function concepts.

commonMistakes: not having proper base cases (causing infinite recursion)
or not combining results properly. The real pattern is def solve(node

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding binary tree nodes, null values, and accessing children.

explanation.prose: This foundational level covers the basic building blocks of binary trees that are prerequisite for the path sum problem. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains a value and references to its children. The intuition is that trees naturally represent hierarchical relationships, and for path sum problems, we need to navigate from root (top) to leaves (bottom). Key concepts: 1. Node structure: has value, left child pointer, right child pointer. 2. Root: the topmost node. 3. Leaf: node with both children null. 4. Null/None: represents absence of a node. 5. Traversal: visiting nodes in some order. Basic operations include: creating nodes, accessing node.value, checking if node.left is null, etc. Time/space not applicable at this basic level.

not checking for null before accessing children

approaches.name: Iterative DFS with Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to perform depth-first search iteratively, tracking node values and cumulative sums. This avoids recursion overhead and is useful for deep trees.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Path Sum Exists Using Iterative DFS with Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if any root-to-leaf path sum equals target using iterative DFS with stack.

explanation.prose: This problem requires checking if there exists a root-to-leaf path in a binary tree where the sum of node values equals a given target. The iterative DFS approach uses a stack to simulate depth-first traversal without recursion, which avoids recursion overhead and is efficient for deep trees. The intuition is to traverse the tree while tracking both the current node and the cumulative sum from the root to that node. By pushing pairs (node, current_sum) onto the stack, we can explore all paths systematically. When we reach a leaf node (both children are null), we check if the current_sum equals the target. If yes, return true; otherwise, continue exploring other paths. If all paths are exhausted without finding a match, return false. The algorithm starts by pushing the root with its value as the initial sum. Then, while the stack is not empty, pop a pair, check if it's a leaf and if the sum matches the target. If not, push the right child (if exists) with updated sum, then the left child (if exists) with updated sum (this order ensures left-first traversal).

connectsTo.reason: by implementing the complete traversal logic

timeComplexity.note: as each node is visited once

spaceComplexity.note: where h is the height of the tree, as the stack holds at most one path from root to leaf

explanation.codePattern: Use a stack of tuples (node, current_sum), initialize with (root, root

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack with (root=5, sum=5). Stack: [(5,5)].
Step 2: Pop (5,5). Not leaf. Push right child 8 with sum=5+8=13. Push left child 4 with sum=5+4=9. Stack: [(4,9), (8,13)].
Step 3: Pop (4,9). Not leaf. Push right child null. Push left child 11 with sum=9+11=20. Stack: [(8,13), (11,20)].
Step 4: Pop (11,20). Not leaf. Push right child 2 with sum=20+2=22. Push left child 7 with sum=20+7=27. Stack: [(8,13), (7,27), (2,22)].
Step 5: Pop (2,22). Leaf node (both children null). Sum=22 equals target=22. Return true.

Step 1: Initialize stack with (root=1, sum=1). Stack: [(1,1)].
Step 2: Pop (1,1). Not leaf. Push right child 3 with sum=1+3=4. Push left child 2 with sum=1+2=3. Stack: [(2,3), (3,4)].
Step 3: Pop (2,3). Leaf node. Sum=3 not equal target=5. Stack: [(3,4)].
Step 4: Pop (3,4). Leaf node. Sum=4 not equal target=5. Stack empty.
Step 5: Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative DFS Traversal with Stack for Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement iterative depth-first traversal of a binary tree using a stack, tracking nodes and cumulative sums.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine focuses on performing iterative DFS on a binary tree using a stack, which is the core mechanism for the path sum problem. The intuition is to simulate recursive DFS without recursion by explicitly managing a stack data structure. This avoids call stack overhead and provides more control over traversal. The algorithm involves pushing the root node onto the stack along with an initial sum (the root's value). Then, in a loop, we pop a node-sum pair, process it, and push its children with updated sums. The key is to push the right child first and then the left child to ensure left-first traversal (since stack is LIFO). This traversal visits all nodes in a depth-first manner, allowing us to check conditions at leaf nodes.

connectsTo.reason: by providing the traversal framework needed to check path sums

timeComplexity.note: as each node is pushed and popped once

commonMistakes: incorrect stack initialization for null root

Step 3: Pop (2,3). Process node 2. No children. Stack: [(3,4)].
Step 4: Pop (3,4). Process node 3. No children. Stack empty.
Traversal complete.

Step 4: Pop (2,10). Process node 2. No children. Stack: [(7,12), (4,12)].
Step 5: Pop (4,12). Process node 4. No children. Stack: [(7,12)].
Step 6: Pop (7,12). Process node 7. No children. Stack empty.
Traversal complete.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Leaf Node and Sum Condition in DFS
ladders.desc: Identify leaf nodes during traversal and verify if cumulative sum equals target.

explanation.prose: This core logic involves two critical checks during DFS traversal: determining if a node is a leaf and comparing the cumulative sum to a target value. The intuition is that in the path sum problem, we only care about paths that end at leaf nodes (nodes with no children), and we need to confirm if the sum along that path matches the target. A leaf node is defined as having both left and right children as null. During iterative DFS, when we pop a node from the stack, we first check if it is a leaf. If it is, we then check if the current cumulative sum equals the target. If both conditions are true, we can immediately return true (path found). If not, we continue traversal by pushing children onto the stack. This logic is efficient because it stops early upon finding a valid path. Time complexity for this check is O(1) per node, but integrated into O(n) traversal.

connectsTo.reason: by adding the condition checks to the traversal routine

commonMistakes: checking only one child) or comparing sum incorrectly

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Use stack data structure to manage nodes and sums during iterative tree traversal.

explanation.prose: This building block focuses on using a stack to perform iterative tree traversal, which is essential for avoiding recursion. The intuition is that a stack (Last-In, First-Out) mimics the call stack in recursive DFS, allowing us to explore depth-first paths explicitly. We store pairs of (node, current_sum) in the stack to track both the node to visit and the cumulative sum from the root to that node. Operations include: push to add a new node-sum pair when exploring children, and pop to retrieve the next node to process. The order of pushing children is crucial: push right child first, then left child, so that left child is processed first (due to stack LIFO). This ensures a left-first DFS traversal. The stack starts with the root and its value, and we loop until the stack is empty. Time complexity for stack operations is O(1) per push/pop, overall O(n).

connectsTo.reason: by providing the data structure to implement the traversal and checks

commonMistakes: using queue instead of stack (which would give BFS)

Step 5: Push left child 3 with sum=5+3=8: stack.append((3,8)). Stack: [(7,12), (3,8)].
Now stack has (3,8) on top.

Step 4: Now node=2, curr_sum=10 available for processing.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Tree Node Children and Values
ladders.desc: Retrieve left child, right child, and value from a binary tree node.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves accessing properties of a binary tree node, which is fundamental for traversal. In a binary tree, each node typically has three attributes: a value (e.g., an integer), a left child (reference to left subtree node), and a right child (reference to right subtree node). The intuition is that to traverse the tree, we need to read the node's value to compute sums and check its children to decide the next steps. For example, to check if a node is a leaf, we verify that both left and right children are null (or None). To push children onto the stack, we access node.left and node.right, and compute new sums by adding their values to the current sum. This operation is performed repeatedly during DFS.

commonMistakes: accessing children without null checks (leading to errors)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Values
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree node is and how null represents absence of children.

explanation.prose: This foundation concept introduces the basic structure of a binary tree node, which is essential for all tree algorithms. A binary tree node is a data structure that contains a value (e.g., a number) and references to two other nodes: left child and right child. These children can be other nodes or null (or None), which indicates that there is no child in that direction. The intuition is that a tree is built from these nodes linked together, starting from a root node. Null values are critical because they define the boundaries of the tree—for example, a leaf node has both children as null. In programming, we often represent null as a special value (like None in Python or null in Java) to signify the absence of a node. Understanding this allows us to traverse the tree by checking if children are null before accessing them. This concept is prerequisite for L4, as accessing children requires knowing what null means.
commonMistakes: treating null as a node with value
or not handling null cases in algorithms

approaches.name: BFS with Queue O(n)
approaches.summary: Perform breadth-first search using a queue, processing nodes level by level and checking path sums at leaf nodes. This explores the tree in a different order than DFS.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree (worst-case O(n))

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS Path Sum Check
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BFS with queue to check if any root-to-leaf path sum equals target

explanation.prose: This solution implements a breadth-first search approach using a queue to traverse the binary tree level by level, checking at each leaf node whether the accumulated path sum equals the target value. The intuition behind BFS is that it explores the tree horizontally rather than vertically, which can be advantageous for certain tree structures or when we want to find the shortest path (though not relevant for this specific problem). We maintain a queue that stores pairs of (node, current_sum), where current_sum represents the sum from root to that node. Starting from the root with sum=root.val, we process nodes level by level. For each node, if it's a leaf (both children are null), we check if current_sum equals target. If yes, return true immediately. Otherwise, for each non-null child, we enqueue it with updated sum (current_sum + child.val). If we exhaust the queue without finding a matching leaf, return false. The algorithm runs in O(n) time since each node is visited once, .

connectsTo.reason: by implementing the complete BFS traversal logic with path sum tracking

spaceComplexity.note: where w is the maximum width of the tree (worst-case O(n) for a complete tree)

commonMistakes: or not updating sums properly when enqueuing children. The BFS order ensures we explore all possible paths
though we might find a solution later than DFS in some cases

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Sum Tracking
ladders.desc: BFS traversal that tracks accumulated sum from root to each node

explanation.prose: This sub-routine implements the core BFS traversal mechanism while maintaining the current path sum for each node. The intuition is that during BFS, we need to preserve the sum information along with node references because unlike DFS where recursion stack naturally maintains path context, BFS requires explicit tracking. We use a queue data structure that stores pairs (node, current_sum). Starting from the root with sum=root.val, we process nodes in FIFO order. For each dequeued node, we examine its children. For each non-null child, we calculate new_sum = current_sum + child.val and enqueue (child, new_sum). This ensures that when we reach any node, current_sum contains the exact sum of values from root to that node. The algorithm runs in O(n) time as each node is processed once, This feeds into L0 by providing the traversal framework; L0 adds the leaf-checking logic.
commonMistakes: forgetting to initialize with root's value
or incorrectly calculating sums. The pairing is crucial for correct sum propagation through levels

examples.trace:
Initial queue: [(1,1)]
Dequeue (1,1): enqueue left child (2,1+2=3) → queue: [(2,3)]
Dequeue (2,3): enqueue right child (3,1+3=4) → queue: [(3,4)]
Dequeue (3,4): no children, queue empty
Final sums tracked: root→1=1, root→2=3, root→3=4

Initial queue: [(5,5)]
Dequeue (5,5): enqueue left child (3,5+3=8) and right child (8,5+8=13) → queue: [(3,8), (8,13)]
Dequeue (3,8): no children
Dequeue (8,13): no children
Queue empty
Final sums: root→5=5, root→3=8, root→8=13

ladders.title: Core Logic — Leaf Node Identification in BFS
ladders.desc: Identifying leaf nodes during BFS traversal

explanation.prose: The core insight is recognizing leaf nodes during BFS traversal. A leaf node in a binary tree is defined as a node with no children (both left and right are null). In BFS, when we process a node from the queue, we need to check if it's a leaf to potentially evaluate the path sum. The intuition is simple: leaves represent end points of root-to-leaf paths, so only at leaves do we have complete paths to check. During BFS, we process nodes level by level, so leaves will be encountered after all their ancestors. The algorithm: when dequeuing a node, check if node.left is null AND node.right is null. If true, it's a leaf. This check .

connectsTo.reason: by adding the condition that triggers sum verification; L1 handles the traversal and sum tracking, while this adds the leaf detection

commonMistakes: incorrectly identifying leaves (checking only one null child
or confusing leaves with other nodes. In BFS

examples.trace:
BFS order: 1, 2, 3
Process 1: left=2, right=3 → not leaf
Process 2: left=null, right=null → leaf
Process 3: left=null, right=null → leaf

BFS order: 5, 3, 8, 1, 4
Process 5: left=3, right=8 → not leaf
Process 3: left=1, right=4 → not leaf
Process 8: left=null, right=null → leaf
Process 1: left=null, right=null → leaf
Process 4: left=null, right=null → leaf
